name: Centre Maintenance and Cleaning

description:
The Middle Length Lam-Rim (Lam rim 'bring ba in Tibetan) is one of three texts on the "Stages of the Path to Enlightenment" composed by Lama Tsongkhapa (1357–1419), the great Tibetan Buddhist master and founder of the Gelug school. The three versions — Great, Middle Length, and Small — are arranged by scope and depth, and this middle one strikes a balance between the comprehensive elaboration of the Great Lam-Rim and the more condensed Small version.

The text is a systematic guide leading practitioners of all three levels of spiritual capacity — lesser, intermediate, and great beings — through the progressive stages of the Buddhist path toward full enlightenment. It draws on the lineages of both Nāgārjuna and Asaṅga, the two great trailblazers of Madhyamaka and Yogācāra philosophy, and is rooted especially in Atiśa's Lamp for the Path to Enlightenment (Bodhipathapradīpa).

The text you have is a 316-page English translation by Philip Quarcoo, produced as part of the FPMT Basic Program(Foundation for the Preservation of the Mahayana Tradition) and finalised in May 2012. It also incorporates additional structural outlines by Trijang Rinpoche, one of the great Gelug masters of the 20th century, printed in smaller type alongside Tsongkhapa's original outlines.

The content moves through six broad chapters: an introduction establishing the text's authority and how it should be studied; the path for lesser beings (covering impermanence, karma, refuge, and the lower realms); the path for intermediate beings (the shortcomings of cyclic existence and the road to liberation); the Mahāyāna path of great beings (bodhicitta, the six perfections, and the exchange of self and others); the meditative practices of calm abiding (śamatha) and special insight (vipaśyanā); and a concluding pointer toward Vajrayāna practice.

Given your connection to CKSL and its Tibetan Buddhist context, this would be a rich study text — it's essentially Tsongkhapa laying out the entire Buddhist path in a structured, accessible form.

Zoom Link - https://us02web.zoom.us/j/81481048676?pwd=Sn4IgBwT5TMOayvaO9Smmcbt1w53SB.1

As CKSL holds His Holiness the Dalai Lama as a primary guru, then in accordance with his wishes and advice, we request that no students actively involved in Dorje Shugden practice attend the centre.

name: Middle Length Lamrim - 7 ONLINE - Venerable  Gyalten Lekden

description:
Special Teaching: The Vajra Cutter Sutra A four-session teaching series with Venerable Tenzin Namjong At Choe Khor Sum Ling, Bengaluru
Sessions:

•⁠  ⁠Session 1 — Saturday, 13 June 2026 | 5:00 pm – 7:00 pm
•⁠  ⁠Session 2 — Sunday, 14 June 2026 | 10:00 am – 12:30 pm
•⁠  ⁠Session 3 — Saturday, 4 July 2026 | 5:00 pm – 7:00 pm
•⁠  ⁠Session 4 — Sunday, 5 July 2026 | 10:00 am – 12:30 pm

Free and open to all. Everyone welcome.

About the Vajra Cutter Sutra
The Vajra Cutter Sutra — known in Sanskrit as the Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra, or the Diamond Sutra — is one of the most celebrated texts in the Mahāyāna Buddhist tradition. Presented as a dialogue between the Buddha and his disciple Subhuti, it goes to the very heart of the teachings on emptiness and the nature of reality.

The title itself is illuminating: vajra means diamond or thunderbolt — something that cuts through all things without itself being cut. The sutra's wisdom is like that: sharp, penetrating, and liberating. It invites us to examine our deepest assumptions about self, phenomena, and experience — and in doing so, dissolves the grasping that lies at the root of suffering.

Why study it?
The Vajra Cutter Sutra is not merely a philosophical text — it is a living practice. The Buddha himself describes the immeasurable merit of studying, memorising, and sharing even a few lines of this sutra. Its teachings directly address the mind that clings: to a self, to outcomes, to ideas of spiritual achievement. Working through it with a qualified teacher deepens one's understanding of emptiness and opens the heart to compassion in ways that transform daily life.

For those new to the sutra, this teaching offers a guided and accessible entry. For those who have encountered it before, Venerable Namjong's commentary will open fresh dimensions of meaning. Simply encountering the Vajra Cutter is considered deeply fortunate.

About the Teacher: Venerable Tenzin Namjong
Born and raised in Hawaii, Venerable Tenzin Namjong followed a wide and sincere spiritual path before finding his home in the Tibetan Buddhist tradition — drawn there through the kindness of His Holiness the Dalai Lama and Lama Zopa Rinpoche. His earlier years of study in both Zen and Theravada traditions give him an unusually broad perspective on Buddhist practice and philosophy.

Venerable Namjong completed 17 years of the traditional Geshe Studies Programme at Sera Je Monastery in South India, undertaking extensive training in Buddhist philosophy, debate, and practice. He also holds a Bachelor of Arts in Philosophy from Princeton University. Following the advice of Lama Zopa Rinpoche, he now dedicates himself primarily to meditation retreat and practice. Venerable Namjong is a registered teacher of the FPMT (Foundation for the Preservation of the Mahayana Tradition) and a beloved regular teacher at Choe Khor Sum Ling.

This teaching is free and open to everyone. No registration required. Simply come with an open mind and an open heart.

description:
BUDDHISM IN A NUTSHELL

Essentials for Practice & Study

New to Buddhism? This is where to begin.

Buddhism in a Nutshell is a warm, accessible, and beautifully structured introduction to Tibetan Buddhist philosophy and practice. Across five sessions, you will explore the life of Shakyamuni Buddha, the Four Noble Truths, the nature of mind and karma, and the three principal aspects of the path to enlightenment — alongside five guided meditations designed for beginners.
